what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard item that lists six overlooked capabilities needed for a voice-first operator experience, covering voice comments, eyes-free review, interruptible TTS, parallel touch fallback, motor ergonomics, and safe voice confirmation.
observations: The item is typed as a block and sourced from claude_code
It links to board://item-60542ab3 via part_of
It contains six numbered requirements for a voice-first operator
It treats voice notes as first-class comment bodies, storing audio and auto-transcribing into envelope text
It proposes an eyes-free review mode where the user says 'comment here' to anchor a note at the current read position
It insists voice and touch remain co-equal, simultaneously available channels rather than modes
It calls for tunable long-press duration and thumb-walk travel, 44pt+ tap targets, and bottom-thumb-arc placement
It requires a glanceable confirmation before sending voice-initiated batches, while allowing lower-ceremony single-comment send
standards: Voice note as a first-class comment body that keeps both audio and transcript
The principle that voice and touch are co-equal, always-live channels
Eyes-free review mode that removes the screen from the capture phase
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item YAML frontmatter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, timestamps, and history
Uses a numbered list with bold headings for each requirement
Mixes user-facing requirement, rationale, and implementation hints in each bullet
References external context as 'MSG2' and 'the V' without defining them in the file
concerns: No explicit priority, acceptance criteria, or sequencing for the six items
Assumes blob:// audio storage exists without stating format, size, retention, or privacy constraints
Does not address consent or notification when voice recordings are shared
Implementation guidance and rationale are interleaved, which may complicate extraction
Claims these are 'additions nobody else will raise' without supporting evidence in the file
notes: A voice-first accessibility brief adding six under-specified VUI and motor-ergonomic requirements to the operator experience.
questions: What is the exact relationship between this item and board://item-60542ab3?
What product or interface do 'the V' and 'MSG2' refer to?
What is 'envelope text' in this context?
What audio format, duration limit, and retention policy apply to voice notes?
How is the 'current read position' determined during eyes-free review?
What constitutes a 'glanceable' confirmation and where does it appear?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches content: it enumerates six additions needed for a voice-first operator, exactly as the title describes.
apparent_themes: Safety and confirmation for high-stakes voice commands
Preservation of intent beyond plain text
intention_signals: To elevate voice from an attachment or alternate mode into a primary, parallel interaction channel, while embedding accessibility and ergonomic safeguards so a user can capture nuanced feedback without relying on sight or precise touch.
novelty: It explicitly frames voice and touch as co-equal, always-available channels rather than mutually exclusive modes, and pairs audio preservation with transcription so tone and emphasis are not lost.
contribution: A concrete checklist of voice-first and motor-ergonomic requirements—first-class voice comments, eyes-free review, barge-in TTS, parallel touch fallback, tunable motor settings, and glanceable voice-send confirmation—that a fused system would retain as design constraints.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item listing six voice-first operator requirements—first-class voice comments, eyes-free review, interruptible TTS, parallel touch fallback, tunable motor ergonomics, and glanceable confirmation—for an accessibility-oriented product brief.
intention_for_embedding: To surface overlooked voice UI and motor-ergonomic needs so the operator can capture and send feedback hands-free and eyes-free without sacrificing safety or touch accessibility.
